items.q: Why does benzene undergo electrophilic SUBSTITUTION but alkenes undergo electrophilic ADDITION?
items.correct: Substitution preserves benzene's stable delocalised pi system; addition would destroy aromaticity at a large energetic cost.
items.misconception: Benzene reacts the same way as alkenes — by addition across one of its double bonds.
items.random_wrong: Benzene cannot react with electrophiles at all.
items.why: Students see C=C in both structures and expect identical mechanisms, missing that delocalisation fundamentally changes the energetics.

items.q: How do alkenes typically react with bromine?
items.correct: By electrophilic addition — bromine adds across the double bond to form a dibromoalkane.
items.misconception: Bromine substitutes for a hydrogen on the alkene, releasing HBr.
items.random_wrong: The alkene polymerises with bromine to form a long chain.
items.why: Students see 'reaction with halogen' and default to the substitution they learned for alkanes, not recognising that the C=C changes the mechanism.

items.q: What does a curly arrow in an organic mechanism represent?
items.correct: The movement of a pair of electrons from where they are to where they end up.
items.misconception: A curly arrow shows the movement of an atom from one position to another.
items.random_wrong: A curly arrow shows the direction in which heat is flowing in the reaction.
items.why: Curly arrows look like they're pointing to atoms, so students think they describe atomic motion rather than electron pair movement.

items.q: Test for C=C with bromine water?
items.why: Correct answer: Orange decolourised. “Orange to green” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
